title: Campaign Ad

jokes.text: I don't know why they call it a 'cam-pain.' Because up until now it's been a 'cam-pleasure.'
jokes.setup: Bobby explaining how his campaign is going great
jokes.punchline: The forced pun on 'campaign' and 'pleasure'
jokes.explanation: Very obvious wordplay that establishes Bobby as earnest but not clever. The groan-worthy nature fits his character.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: My campaign is going great. I'm having the time of my life. You could say I'm really enjoying the campaign... pleasure.

jokes.text: I am a lifelong government bureaucrat who's well-versed in the issues. And those are the kind of sexy qualifications that win elections.
jokes.setup: Leslie contrasting herself with attractive, charming Bobby
jokes.punchline: Calling bureaucratic qualifications 'sexy'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie line - she genuinely believes bureaucratic expertise IS sexy, which is both ironic and completely in character.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Bobby's got the looks, the charm, the charisma. But you know what I've got? A comprehensive understanding of municipal code, a working knowledge of the Parks Department budget, and I've read every single binder in the planning office. And that, my friends, is sexy.

jokes.text: So do you want me to sign that to miss lope or just Nesney?
jokes.setup: Bobby offering to sign something for Leslie
jokes.punchline: Completely mangling her name despite just being introduced
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character establishment - Bobby is so privileged he doesn't really listen to people. The specific mispronunciations are perfectly ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Of course, I'd be happy to sign something for you, uh... Leslee? Is it Leslee?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: It's Leslie.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Right, right, Leslie. L-E-S-L-I-E-E.

jokes.text: Oh, no, you guys are wrong. I'm running unopposed. I'm pretty sure I'm running unopposed, right?
jokes.setup: Being told Leslie is also running for city council
jokes.punchline: Bobby's complete ignorance that he has opposition despite being told directly
jokes.explanation: Shows Bobby's obliviousness perfectly - he literally can't process that someone would run against him.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Wait, Leslie Knope is running for city council? Against me?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: I didn't know that.

jokes.text: Hey, Ann, are you still a nurse or did they fire you because you slept with all the doctors?
jokes.setup: April greeting Ann
jokes.punchline: Casually asking if Ann was fired for sleeping with doctors
jokes.explanation: Perfectly April - inappropriate, delivered deadpan, and shows her weird antagonism toward Ann.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: Hey Ann, were you fired for sleeping with doctors?

jokes.text: Just got a headache. And I'm seeing double. And I got a song stuck in my head, and my teeth hurt. Also I'm hungry.
jokes.setup: Ann asking what's wrong with Andy
jokes.punchline: Listing increasingly unrelated 'symptoms' ending with basic hunger
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy - mixing legitimate concerns with basic human needs as if they're all medical symptoms.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: My stomach hurts, my head hurts, my heart hurts. I think I'm dying, Ann. I'm pretty sure this is what dying feels like.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: Andy, you're not dying.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: How do you know? You're not a doctor. Oh wait, you are a nurse. But that's basically the same thing, right? My left eye won't stop twitching. I think I have a brain tumor. Also, I'm pretty sure I saw a ghost this morning.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: You saw a ghost?

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: It was wearing a white sheet. Turned out to be a bed sheet. From my bed. I couldn't find it this morning and got really upset. Also, I haven't eaten since yesterday.

jokes.text: Okay, well, some of those things are symptoms and some of them are just being a person.
jokes.setup: Andy's ridiculous list of 'symptoms'
jokes.punchline: Ann's dry assessment that some are just normal human experiences
jokes.explanation: Perfect straight-man response. Ann's medical expertise makes the distinction between symptoms and 'being a person' funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: I've been experiencing some troubling symptoms. Occasional headaches, fatigue, and sometimes my left arm feels tingly.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: Andy, those are just normal human experiences. You're not dying.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: But what about the tingly arm?

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: You probably slept on it wrong.

jokes.text: Oh, yes, technically I did smash my head area into the wall area.
jokes.setup: Being asked if he hit his head after saying no
jokes.punchline: Using 'area' unnecessarily for both head and wall
jokes.explanation: Andy's weird speech patterns - adding 'area' to make it sound more technical/medical while being completely wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: No, I didn't hit my head.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: I hit the wall in the head area with my head area.

jokes.text: Yeah, he was hanging up his gold record.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining he hit his head
jokes.punchline: April's casual mention of the absurd detail that causes pride in the middle of injury
jokes.explanation: April's deadpan delivery makes Andy's ridiculous accomplishment even funnier in context of his injury.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I hit my head pretty hard on the swing set.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Yeah, you were out for like twenty seconds. It was awesome.

jokes.text: Point is, I have a gold record.
jokes.setup: Discussing his head injury
jokes.punchline: Andy redirecting to brag about his achievement despite being hurt
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy priorities - even while discussing his injury, he can't resist bragging about his music success.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I got hit in the head pretty bad, but you know what? I've been trying to remember things better, and I actually remembered my PIN code on the first try today.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: So who's the winner now?

jokes.text: Neither of your asses are that smart because insurance is for if something bad happens to your car. P.S. I haven't had it in years.
jokes.setup: Discussion about health insurance
jokes.punchline: April thinking insurance is only for cars, then revealing she doesn't have that either
jokes.explanation: Great escalation - April's confident wrong explanation followed by admission she doesn't even have car insurance.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: Wait, health insurance? I thought insurance was just for cars.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: I don't have that either.

jokes.text: This is exactly how I dreamed it would be when I was a kid. Except I wasn't 70 points behind and my campaign manager was Mr. belvedere.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing her dream campaign scenario
jokes.punchline: The specific detail about Mr. Belvedere as campaign manager
jokes.explanation: Perfectly Leslie - having specific, earnest childhood political dreams that included a sitcom butler as campaign manager.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: In my dream scenario, Mr. Belvedere is my campaign manager.

jokes.text: I've personally put out several local fires at no cost to the taxpayer.
jokes.setup: Chris asking if Ron considered cutting the fire department
jokes.punchline: Ron's matter-of-fact claim about personally fighting fires
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron - his extreme self-reliance taken to absurd but believable lengths. Delivered with complete sincerity.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: I have not considered it. I volunteer at the Fire Department. I fight fires.

dialogue.speaker: Chris Traeger
dialogue.line: You do?

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: No. But if there's ever a fire, I will be there with a gun and some increasingly angry buckets of water.

jokes.text: Are you the kind of doctor who gets hooked on his own drugs? I bet you are.
jokes.setup: Meeting a doctor
jokes.punchline: April's immediate, confident accusation about drug addiction
jokes.explanation: Classic April rudeness - making inappropriate assumptions and stating them as fact with confidence.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: Are you a drug addict?

jokes.text: I was reading an encyclopedia, and I tripped or 'fell over' and hit my head or brain helmet.
jokes.setup: Doctor asking what happened
jokes.punchline: Andy's unnecessarily complex explanation with air quotes and calling his head a 'brain helmet'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Andy moment - 'brain helmet' is wonderfully stupid and original. The air quotes around 'fell over' suggest he thinks it's technical jargon.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Well, I was doing this thing where I was like, 'protecting my brain helmet,' you know? And I put my hands up like this, and I guess I didn't realize how hard I was gonna hit myself. So I was just like, 'whoosh,' and then my brain helmet made contact with the wall, and that's when I knew it was bad because usually my brain helmet is pretty 'indestructible,' but apparently it's not as indestructible as I thought.

jokes.text: Yeah, he sneezed and smacked his head against the wall.
jokes.setup: Andy's elaborate story about reading an encyclopedia
jokes.punchline: April's simple, deflating explanation of what actually happened
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Andy's grandiose story. April's matter-of-fact delivery makes the mundane reality funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Barton
dialogue.line: I was reading this encyclopedia and I got so lost in it, I didn't even realize three hours had passed. It was incredible!

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: You fell asleep on the couch.

jokes.text: Nope, I've got a weird rash in my knee pit area. And my tongue, on this side, doesn't taste anything anymore.
jokes.setup: Doctor asking if anything else is bothering him after saying no
jokes.punchline: Immediately listing multiple bizarre health issues including 'knee pit area'
jokes.explanation: 'Knee pit area' is wonderfully Andy - trying to sound medical while being completely wrong. The escalation from 'nope' to multiple issues is great.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Is there anything else bothering you?

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: You sure?

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Well, actually, now that you mention it, my knee pit area has been really itchy, I've been having these weird dreams where I'm falling, and sometimes when I sneeze, a little bit of pee comes out.

jokes.text: Sometimes when I walk my ankles make, like, a weird rattlesnake sound.
jokes.setup: Continuing his list of health problems
jokes.punchline: The specific comparison to a rattlesnake sound
jokes.explanation: The rattlesnake comparison is wonderfully specific and absurd. Andy's attempt at describing medical symptoms in his own terms.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: My doctor says I have the health of a sixty-year-old man. I'm thirty-four. He also says I sound like a rattlesnake when I breathe. I don't know what that means, but it doesn't sound good.

jokes.text: I once ate a Twix with the wrapper on it, and I've never seen the wrapper come out.
jokes.setup: Continuing his medical concerns
jokes.punchline: The gross revelation about eating candy wrapper and tracking its digestive progress
jokes.explanation: Perfectly disgusting Andy logic. The fact that he's been monitoring for the wrapper's exit is both gross and hilarious.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: So I ate a candy wrapper yesterday, and I've been tracking its progress through my digestive system. I'm pretty sure I felt it pass through my stomach around 2 PM.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Gergich
dialogue.line: Andy, you're not supposed to eat the wrapper.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Well, I know that now, Jerry. But the important thing is, I'm documenting the journey. By tomorrow, it'll be out of my system completely, and I'll have a full timeline to present to my doctor.

jokes.text: Also I've swallowed every piece of gum that I've chewed for the past 25 years.
jokes.setup: Already establishing his poor health habits
jokes.punchline: The extreme duration - 25 years of swallowed gum
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from the Twix wrapper. The 25-year timeframe suggests he's been doing this since childhood.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: I've been swallowing gum for 25 years. The doctor said I'm gonna die, but I'm gonna die with the cleanest insides.

jokes.text: I broke my thumb on the way over here. Just fix me.
jokes.setup: Long list of existing health problems
jokes.punchline: Casually adding a broken thumb and demanding a total fix
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending to the escalation. 'Just fix me' is so simple and childlike after the litany of problems.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: So I've got high cholesterol, type 2 diabetes, and a really bad back. Oh, and I broke my thumb. Can you just... fix all of it? Like, everything?

jokes.text: Hey, I'm Bobby Newport. I'm a regular guy. I like dogs. I'm here with my persian greyhound raclette, who was given to me by my buddy, the pretender to the crown of alsace-Lorraine.
jokes.setup: Bobby claiming to be a 'regular guy'
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicting this with an absurdly fancy dog with royal connections
jokes.explanation: Perfect encapsulation of Bobby's privilege blindness. The specificity of 'pretender to the crown of Alsace-Lorraine' is brilliantly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: I'm just a regular guy, you know? I shop at the same stores everyone else does.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: In fact, my dog is a rescue dog. Well, he was rescued from the Queen of England's personal kennel, but still.

jokes.text: Nah-uh, I always go negative. Even if I like the guy I go negative. Keeps 'em interested.
jokes.setup: Ben suggesting Donna might have problems going negative
jokes.punchline: Donna's matter-of-fact approach to always being negative as a strategy
jokes.explanation: Perfect Donna - confident, strategic, and completely unbothered by conventional morality about negative campaigning.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: Donna, I think you might have some issues going negative.

dialogue.speaker: Donna Meagle
dialogue.line: Ben, I don't have issues. I have a strategy. If you assume the worst, you're never disappointed. And occasionally, you get pleasantly surprised.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: That's not healthy.

dialogue.speaker: Donna Meagle
dialogue.line: It's worked for me for 55 years.

jokes.text: Oh, my God, this magic marker smells like cherries.
jokes.setup: Supposedly having a breakdown about negative campaigning
jokes.punchline: Ann being completely distracted by pleasant marker smell
jokes.explanation: Great misdirection - sets up Ann as having a crisis but she's just pleasantly distracted. Shows her fundamental niceness.

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: I can't believe how negative this campaign has gotten. It's awful. Leslie and I used to be friends, and now we're just... I don't know, it feels like we're enemies and... wait, is this a Crayola marker?

dialogue.speaker: Ann Perkins
dialogue.line: Oh my God, this smells amazing. What color is this? Burnt sienna? I love burnt sienna.

jokes.text: Dam's dead. Have a nice day.
jokes.setup: Public works official asking about the river dam project
jokes.punchline: Ron's blunt, cheerful dismissal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron economy and directness. The cheerful 'have a nice day' after destroying someone's dream is beautifully cold.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: I don't care about the river dam project. I don't care about rivers. I don't care about dams. And I certainly don't care about your concerns. Good day.

jokes.text: The important thing is the dam is never happening and your dream has been crushed.
jokes.setup: Continuing to explain the cancelled dam project
jokes.punchline: Ron's matter-of-fact acknowledgment of crushing dreams
jokes.explanation: Ron's complete lack of empathy delivered with bureaucratic matter-of-factness. The explicit acknowledgment of dream-crushing is wonderfully dark.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: I crushed your dreams. That's what I do. I crush dreams and build dams. And we're all out of dams.

jokes.text: When I bet on horses, I never lose. Why? I bet on all the horses.
jokes.setup: Tom explaining his strategy for picking sides
jokes.punchline: Revealing he bets on every horse to guarantee winning
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom logic - finding a way to never technically lose while missing the entire point of competition.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Here's my strategy: I bet on every horse. That way, no matter which one wins, I'm a winner.

jokes.text: Well, you wanted to go negative. You got the biggest negative in the world, buddy.
jokes.setup: Assigning Jerry to Ben's team
jokes.punchline: Leslie's burn on Jerry being the 'biggest negative'
jokes.explanation: Good setup/punchline structure playing on the negative ad concept. Standard Jerry abuse but well-integrated into the plot.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Jerry, you're going to be on Ben's team.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Gergich
dialogue.line: Oh, okay.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Yeah, I figure if anyone's going to be a big negative, it might as well be you.

jokes.text: Barack Obama said, 'yes we can,' and now he's president. Ben says, 'no we shouldn't,' and now he's working for his girlfriend.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining why positive is better than negative
jokes.punchline: The comparison between Obama's success and Ben's current situation
jokes.explanation: Clever parallel structure between Obama and Ben. The implication about their relationship dynamic is sharp and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Look, being positive is better than being negative. Obama was positive, and he became president. Ben is negative, and he's stuck in a mid-level government job.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: I'm not negative, I'm realistic.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: That's what negative people say.

jokes.text: E, h, 4, m. Potato shape. Coffee mug shape. Smudge, smudge, middle finger, smudge. The rest are all smudges.
jokes.setup: Andy taking an eye exam
jokes.punchline: His creative interpretation of the eye chart including 'middle finger'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Andy interpretation of an eye chart. 'Middle finger' suggests he thinks the doctor is flipping him off via the chart.

dialogue.speaker: Optometrist
dialogue.line: Can you read the next line?

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Yeah, it says... W, uh... that's a middle finger, right? And then an E, another middle finger, and then... is that a Q or a 9?

dialogue.speaker: Optometrist
dialogue.line: Those are all the letter M.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Oh, wow. Your font is really aggressive.

jokes.text: No, he's not getting glasses. I like his face the way it is. If you get glasses, I'll divorce you.
jokes.setup: Doctor saying Andy needs glasses
jokes.punchline: April's immediate threat of divorce over glasses
jokes.explanation: Pure April - weirdly controlling and dramatic about something minor. The extreme response is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: If you get glasses, I'm divorcing you.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: What? Why?

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: You'll look like a nerd.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: I am a nerd.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: Exactly. And I married you despite that, not because of it.

jokes.text: Too handsome for glasses, no offense.
jokes.setup: April's objection to glasses
jokes.punchline: Andy's agreement framed as concern for the doctor's feelings
jokes.explanation: Andy's vanity combined with his weird politeness to the doctor. The 'no offense' suggests he thinks being less handsome is offensive.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I don't like glasses.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, I don't like them either. I'm worried the doctor's gonna be really upset.

jokes.text: I gotta admit, his hair game's on point. Gotta find out what kind of product he puts up in his 'herr.'
jokes.setup: Commenting on Bobby Newport
jokes.punchline: Tom's pronunciation of 'hair' as 'herr' while trying to sound cool
jokes.explanation: Tom trying to be cool with slang but mispronouncing it. Fits his character of trying too hard to be trendy.

dialogue.speaker: Tom Haverford
dialogue.line: Bobby Newport's got great herr. I mean hair. No wait, herr. That's how they say it in Europe, so it's cooler.

jokes.text: Bobby Newport's never had a real job in his life. - Bobby Newport's never had a real job in his life. Bobby Newport. - Bobby. - Bobby. Bobby. Newport.
jokes.setup: Jerry doing voiceover for attack ad
jokes.punchline: His complete inability to deliver the line properly, devolving into just saying the name
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jerry incompetence. The escalation from full sentence to just repeating the name is beautifully absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Gergich
dialogue.line: Congressman Newport. Congressman Newport. Congress... man... Newport.

jokes.text: All right, now we're just wasting time, Jerry.
jokes.setup: Jerry's complete breakdown in voiceover
jokes.punchline: Ben's understated reaction to the disaster
jokes.explanation: Good straight-man reaction. Ben's diplomatic way of saying Jerry completely ruined everything.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Gergich
dialogue.line: I can't believe this is happening. Everything's falling apart. My career is over, my reputation is ruined, and I don't know how I'm going to face anyone ever again.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: It's gonna be fine, Jerry.

jokes.text: A cucumber.
jokes.setup: Chris asking what Ron will order, mentioning something raw and cucumber-based
jokes.punchline: Ron's literal interpretation - just ordering a plain cucumber
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron moment - taking Chris's fancy description and ordering the most basic possible thing. Economy and character integration are flawless.

dialogue.speaker: Chris Traeger
dialogue.line: What are you going to order, Ron? Something raw and cucumber-based?

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: I'll have a cucumber.

jokes.text: I don't know what happened. I declined his invitation. He started laughing. And the next thing I knew, we were at lunch. Did he drug me?
jokes.setup: Ron confused about how he ended up at lunch with Chris
jokes.punchline: His genuine bewilderment and suspicion about being drugged
jokes.explanation: Great Ron confusion at social manipulation. His serious consideration of being drugged shows how baffled he is by Chris's persistence.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: I don't remember agreeing to this lunch. Did you drug me?

dialogue.speaker: Chris Traeger
dialogue.line: What? No, Ron, I didn't drug you.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: Then how did I get here? I have no recollection of the last two hours.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: And my drink tastes like it has a memory-altering substance in it.

jokes.text: As you can see, I'm in favor of a lot of things, like hot dogs. Right, Charlie?
jokes.setup: Leslie's positive ad listing things she supports
jokes.punchline: The bizarre inclusion of hot dogs and apparent conversation with someone off-camera
jokes.explanation: Leslie's earnest but poorly executed ad. The random hot dog mention and mysterious Charlie reference show her ad-making inexperience.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: I'm Leslie Knope, and I support the following things: a strong public sector, women's rights, environmental protection, education, affordable housing, and hot dogs.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Wait, why did I say hot dogs?

dialogue.speaker: Off-screen voice
dialogue.line: You like hot dogs!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: That's not a political position!

jokes.text: Leslie is so good at so many things and so bad at that.
jokes.setup: Watching Leslie's terrible ad
jokes.punchline: Ben's diplomatic but honest assessment
jokes.explanation: Ben's loving but frank assessment. Good character moment showing his affection and honesty about Leslie's abilities.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: Look, I'm not going to lie to you. That was... that was really bad.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: Like, impressively bad. I didn't know it was possible to be that bad.

jokes.text: I got my ankles microwaved. - X-rayed. They took my blood away to use for science. - Cholesterol tests.
jokes.setup: Andy describing medical procedures
jokes.punchline: His wrong terminology corrected by April ('microwaved' for X-rayed, 'for science' for tests)
jokes.explanation: 'Microwaved' for X-rayed is brilliantly wrong Andy logic. April's corrections show she actually pays attention despite her attitude.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: They microwaved my arm.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: You mean X-rayed.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: That's what I said. They also ran some tests for science.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: For medical reasons.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Yeah, that too.

jokes.text: Some guy looked at my wiener, touched it. That was weird. And that guy wasn't even a doctor.
jokes.setup: Continuing to describe medical procedures
jokes.punchline: The revelation that the person wasn't actually a doctor
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - starts as awkward medical procedure, becomes something much worse. Andy's casual delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Wait, you weren't actually a doctor?

dialogue.speaker: Unknown Character
dialogue.line: No, I'm not a doctor.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: But you've been describing all these medical procedures like you knew what you were talking about!

dialogue.speaker: Unknown Character
dialogue.line: I watched a lot of Grey's Anatomy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Oh my God.

jokes.text: I am Leslie Knope, and I do not approve this message.
jokes.setup: Standard political ad disclaimer
jokes.punchline: Leslie tackling Ben to prevent the negative ad from airing
jokes.explanation: Brilliant physical comedy moment. Leslie literally disapproving the message by stopping it physically. Perfect character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: This ad was paid for by the Committee to Elect Leslie Knope.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: I'm Leslie Knope and I approve this message.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: Wait, no I don't!

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: Leslie, what are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: I'm not letting this ad air. This is a negative ad and I don't do negative ads.

jokes.text: Alright, the good news is, there's only two pieces of bad news.
jokes.setup: Ben trying to deliver news diplomatically
jokes.punchline: The illogical framing of good news being that there's bad news
jokes.explanation: Good attempt at diplomatic messaging that reveals how bad the situation is. Ben's political spin training showing.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: I have some good news and some bad news.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: The good news is, the bad news isn't as bad as it could be.

jokes.text: You were pretty easy to take down.
jokes.setup: Leslie apologizing for tackling him
jokes.punchline: Ben's matter-of-fact assessment of his own lack of physical prowess
jokes.explanation: Ben's self-deprecating honesty about his physical weakness. Good character moment showing his realism.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: I'm so sorry I tackled you.

dialogue.speaker: Ben Wyatt
dialogue.line: It's okay. I mean, I'm not a physical person. I'm more of a... spreadsheet guy.

jokes.text: Let's get you home and get me some ice cream.
jokes.setup: Andy being in pain from medical procedures
jokes.punchline: April's priorities - helping Andy but also getting herself ice cream
jokes.explanation: Perfect April - showing care for Andy while also being selfish. The equal weight given to both goals is funny.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: I would help you, but I'm going to get ice cream first. My priorities are pretty clear.

jokes.text: Oh, I think you're wrong. We have the insurance thing. I believe we're covered.
jokes.setup: Being told they owe $500 despite having insurance
jokes.punchline: Andy's confident ignorance about how insurance works
jokes.explanation: Callbacks to earlier insurance misunderstanding. Andy's confidence despite being completely wrong is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Wait, we have insurance? So why do we owe $500?

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: That's what insurance is for. It's like... it's like a shield made of money that protects you from having to spend money.

jokes.text: Let's go! Dine and dash. Out of the way! Yeah! Whoo-hoo-hoo! Ah! Ah, call an ambulance! A different ambulance than the one I ran into.
jokes.setup: Andy trying to avoid paying the medical bill
jokes.punchline: Attempting to dine and dash from a hospital, then immediately needing medical help
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation and physical comedy. Andy's attempt to avoid medical bills by running away only creates more medical bills. The specific request for a 'different ambulance' is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: I'm not paying this medical bill. I'm just gonna run out of here!

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: They can't catch me, I'm fast.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: Ow! Ow! My leg! I think I pulled something!

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: I need a doctor!

jokes.text: People keep asking me: Bobby, what are you gonna do once you get into office? Um, I'm pretty sure I'll figure it out.
jokes.setup: Bobby being asked about his political platform
jokes.punchline: His complete lack of any plan or preparation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bobby privilege - assuming he can just wing being in office. The casual 'I'll figure it out' shows his complete unpreparedness.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Well, I think the most important thing is... uh... I haven't really thought about it. I mean, I've got some ideas, but they're mostly just things my dad told me to say. Actually, no, I don't even remember those.

jokes.text: This is a great friend of mine. He is a fellow who works in the building and he is named Dennis. Kyle.
jokes.setup: Ron introducing someone to Chris
jokes.punchline: Getting the person's name wrong immediately after introducing him
jokes.explanation: Ron's attempt at manipulation undermined by his complete lack of interest in other people. Calling him a 'great friend' then getting his name wrong is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: Chris, I'd like you to meet Jerry Gergich, our Parks Department... wait, no. Gary. Gary Gergich.

dialogue.speaker: Chris Traeger
dialogue.line: I thought his name was Jerry?

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: It is. I just like to call him Gary to hurt his feelings.

jokes.text: I was eating rice, and Ron made me come up here.
jokes.setup: Being asked about Japanese culture interest
jokes.punchline: The mundane truth about how he ended up there
jokes.explanation: Perfect deflation of Ron's attempted setup. Kyle's honesty about just eating rice and being forced to come undercuts the whole scheme.

dialogue.speaker: Kyle
dialogue.line: I'm not really interested in Japanese culture. I just needed a job.

jokes.text: Come on, give me it. Give me it. Give me it. Just give me the election.
jokes.setup: Bobby asking Leslie to quit the race
jokes.punchline: Devolving into childlike pleading and repetition
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bobby character moment - his privileged entitlement reduced to literal child-like begging. The repetition of 'give me it' is brilliantly immature.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Leslie, please just quit the race.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Just quit. Just quit, quit, quit, quit.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Quit quit quit quit quit quit quit.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Why won't you quit?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Quit!

jokes.text: You know what, I need something to do to get my dad off my back. This seemed easy.
jokes.setup: Leslie asking why he's running for office
jokes.punchline: Bobby's honest admission that politics seemed like an easy way to appease his father
jokes.explanation: Great character revelation - Bobby's complete lack of civic motivation. Thinking politics is 'easy' shows his fundamental misunderstanding.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Well, my dad said I should run for something, and politics seemed easier than a actual business.

jokes.text: How 'bout you quit running, then I win, but then I let you do all the work.
jokes.setup: Bobby trying to convince Leslie to quit
jokes.punchline: His 'compromise' that gives him all the benefits and Leslie all the work
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bobby privilege - thinking this is a reasonable compromise. Shows his complete inability to understand why anyone wouldn't want to do his work for him.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Look, Leslie, I'm a reasonable guy. Let's compromise. You keep doing your job, and I'll take all the credit and the salary.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: That's not a compromise, Bobby. That's just you being you.

jokes.text: That girl is me as a ten-year-old. How'd you do that?
jokes.setup: Bobby asking about the cute girl in Leslie's ad
jokes.punchline: His confusion about how Leslie could be herself as a child
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Bobby moment - he literally cannot understand how someone could have been a child. Shows his complete disconnect from normal human experience.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Wait, how were you yourself as a child?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie Knope
dialogue.line: What do you mean?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Newport
dialogue.line: Well, you're not yourself now.

jokes.text: You could sew up my lip underneath a bridge somewhere. Mmm, so romantic.
jokes.setup: Andy suggesting they treat each other's injuries themselves
jokes.punchline: April finding the idea of primitive medical care under a bridge romantic
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy/April relationship moment - he suggests something absurd and dangerous, she finds it romantic. Captures their weird dynamic perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Andy Dwyer
dialogue.line: We could treat each other's injuries ourselves. It'd be like being our own doctors.

dialogue.speaker: April Ludgate
dialogue.line: That does sound romantic. Like we're living under a bridge, performing surgery with a bottle cap and whatever we find in the dirt.

tmdb_overview: Faced with a tough new opponent, Leslie and Ben disagree on the content of her first campaign ad. Ron is very uncomfortable when Chris tries to befriend him, while Andy and April make a trip to the doctor.
insight: Campaign ad shooting exposes Leslie's control issues through relentless cringe across fifty-five jokes.
